sections.title: Geometry Processing
sections.info: Section Type - IN-PERSON SECTION (campus presence). Special Topics in CS: Geometry Processing. Concepts, representations, and algorithms for processing 3D geometric datasets. Topics include shape representations (e.g., triangle meshes and implicit functions), and the geometry processing pipeline covering the acquisition (e.g., with laser scanning or depth cameras), reconstruction, manipulation, editing, analysis, and fabrication (3D printing) of geometric models. Prerequisites: COMP 2402, (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110), MATH 1007 and MATH 1104. Note: SYSC 2100 is no longer equivalent to COMP 2402. SYSC 2100 may only be used as a prerequisite if taken prior to Fall 2022.

sections.title: Approximation Algorithms
sections.info: Section Type - IN-PERSON SECTION (campus presence). Special Topics in CS: Approximation Algorithms. Based on the famed Vazirani's text, we'll go over a series of methods to create approximate solutions to a range of combinatorial and counting problems, paying particular attention to the link with linear programming. Prerequisites: COMP 2804 and COMP 3804.

sections.title: Algorithms for Data Science
sections.info: Section Type - IN-PERSON SECTION (campus presence). Special Topics in CS: Algorithms for Data Science. Algorithmic techniques to handle (massive/big) data arising from, for example, social media, mobile devices, sensors, financial transactions, etc. Algorithmic techniques may include locality-sensitive hashing, dimensionality reduction, streaming, clustering, VC-dimensions, core sets, link analysis, fixed-parameter tractability, multiple-weight update method, linear programming relaxations, local search, and online algorithms. Prerequisites: COMP 3804 with a minimum grade of B+.

sections.title: Trends in Big Data Management
sections.info: Section Type - IN-PERSON SECTION (campus presence). Special Topics in CS: Trends in Big Data Management. In-depth study of recent research articles in the field of data management, with focus on data integration, internet of things, large scale data management, recommendation systems, text processing, question answering, and large language mode Students will work on a term-long project. Prerequisite: 4th year standing only and COMP 3005 with a minimum grade of A-.

sections.title: Multiagent Aspects of Deep RL
sections.info: Section Type - IN-PERSON SECTION (Campus presence). Special Topics in CS: Multiagent Aspects of Deep RL. Deep (Reinforcement) Learning has been successfully applied in a variety of domains, but the presence of multiple learners (potentially with distinct purposes/optimality criteria) engenders critical non-stationarity in the system. We will study a variety of approaches that deal with, acknowledge and exploit this effect: cooperative learning and its federated point of view, learning to communicate, and behaviour/strategy manipulation. The course will have the format of a reading seminar with a mini-project (we'll aim for publication potential). Prerequisites: COMP 3105 or COMP 3106.

sections.title: Trusted Comp & Emerging Attack
sections.info: Section Type - IN-PERSON SECTION (Campus presence). Special Topics in CS: Trusted Computing and Emerging Attack. The course introduces the paradigm of trusted computing (today aka. and closely related to trusted execution or confidential computing), and the evolution of its corresp- onding attacks and defenses, in both academia and industry. Hardware-back technologies and recent (new) attack vectors of computer program execution will be the focus of class discussions. Prerequisite(s): COMP 3000 with a minimum grade of C-. SYSC 4001 may only be used as a prerequisite if taken prior to Fall 2025. This course has priority registration. Please refer to the Registration Priority List

sections.title: Games for Social Good
sections.info: Section Type - IN-PERSON SECTION (Campus presence). Special Topics in CS: Games for Social Good. This course provides an overview of games and their applications in health, education, and training. Through relevant readings and the creation of prototypes, the course will focus on giving students a practical guide to the process of conceptualizing, designing, and developing games for addressing social good. Topics include an introduction to serious games, history of games for social good, rapid prototyping and playtesting, design analysis frameworks, design thinking, root cause analysis, theory of change, gamification techniques, and game evaluation techniques. Prerequisite: COMP 2501 (with B- or higher). For 3rd and 4th year BCS students only.

sections.title: Honours Project
sections.info: For BCS Honours, BDS (Concentration in AI) & CS/MATH Combined Honours programs only, and permission of the School. Must have 4th year standing. Precludes additional credit for COMP 4906. See carleton.ca/scs/ for permission form, instructions and deadlines. Note: COMP 4905 and 4906 are now optional in all BCS Honours programs.

sections.title: Honours Thesis
sections.info: For BCS Honours & BDS (Concentration in AI) programs only, with minimum CGPA of 9.0 in the major and permission of the School. Must have 4th year standing. This is a full credit course taken in Fall/Winter. Precludes additional credit for COMP 4905. See carleton.ca/scs/ for permission form, instructions and deadlines. Note: COMP 4905 and 4906 are now optional in all BCS Honours programs.

sections.title: Approximation Algorithms
sections.info: Section Type - IN-PERSON SECTION (Campus presence). Special Topics in CS: Approximation Algorithms. Based on the famed Vazirani's text, we'll go over a series of methods to create approximate solutions to a range of combinatorial and counting problems, paying particular attention to the link with linear programming. Priority will be given to OCICS joint institute students and students in thesis-based research programs.

sections.title: Multiagent Systems
sections.info: Section Type - IN-PERSON SECTION (Campus presence). Special Topics in CS: Multiagent Systems. Multiagent systems is a branch of AI that explores the interactions between multiple rational entities, with possibly different information and priorities. This course takes an approach founded on economic game theory. We examine how coordination and cooperation can emerge, and how coordination and cooperation can emerge, and how to design systems to achieve global goals. Students should be with rigorous math and basic Python programming skills. Priority will be given to OCICS joint institute students and students in thesis-based research programs.

sections.title: Games for Social Good

sections.title: Project-based Learning
sections.info: Section Type - IN-PERSON. Provides (1) an environment where TIM students in their 2nd or 3rd term can do research to add value to identifiable identifiable real-world clients and (2) an environment to develop TIM project proposals. The client may be a large firm, a small- or medium-sized company, an entrepreneur, or a Carleton department, faculty, research organization, or support group. Project proposals will follow the established TIM Gate Process for student research and will be presented at a TIM Gate Review.
